title: Psalms 102
subtitle: A Prayer of the Afflicted

heb.paragraph: "A prayer of an afflicted (ʿānî) person who has grown weak and pours out a lament before the LORD" (superscription). The fifth penitential psalm. Days vanish like smoke, bones burn (v.3). The psalmist is like an owl in the desert, a bird alone on a roof (vv.6–7). Enemies use his name as a curse (v.8). He eats ashes and mingles his drink with tears (v.9). "Because of your great wrath... you have thrown me aside" (v.10).

notes.note: "Hear my prayer, LORD. Do not hide your face from me when I am in distress. Turn your ear to me; when I call, answer me quickly." Four urgent requests in two verses.

"My days vanish like smoke; my bones burn like glowing embers. My heart is withered like grass. I am like a desert owl, like an owl among the ruins." The imagery: smoke, embers, withered grass, desert owls. Every image is of desolation.
"My days are like the evening shadow; I wither away like grass." The second grass image. Fading, lengthening, disappearing.

refs.note: Skin shriveled on bones parallels the psalm's withered-grass and forgotten-food imagery.
Skin and bones escaping by the skin of teeth echoes the psalm's wasting description.
Bones out of joint and strength dried up parallel this psalm's physical anguish.

hist.context: Psalm 102 is the fifth of the seven penitential psalms, titled 'a prayer of the afflicted when faint.' The individual's suffering is linked to Zion's desolation (vv.13-22), suggesting an exilic or post-exilic date. The personal crisis is interpreted within the larger national catastrophe of Jerusalem's destruction.

sections.header: Verses 12–28 — “He Will Respond to the Prayer of the Destitute”

heb.gloss: from generation to generation / age after age
heb.paragraph: "But you, LORD, sit enthroned forever; your renown endures through all generations (lədōr wā-dōr)" (v.12). The pivot: from human transience to divine permanence. God will arise and have compassion on Zion (v.13). "He will respond to the prayer of the destitute" (v.17). The future nations will praise God (v.18). Hebrews 1:10–12 quotes vv.25–27 for Christ’s eternality: "In the beginning you laid the foundations of the earth... they will perish, but you remain."

notes.note: "But you, LORD, sit enthroned forever. You will arise and have compassion on Zion, for it is time to show favour to her." The "but you" pivot: human frailty vs divine permanence. The appointed time has come.

"He will respond to the prayer of the destitute; he will not despise their plea. Let this be written for a future generation, that a people not yet created may praise the LORD." The psalmist writes for the unborn. His suffering becomes the next generation’s testimony.

"In the beginning you laid the foundations of the earth; the heavens are the work of your hands. They will perish, but you remain. Like a garment they will be worn out, but you remain the same." Hebrews 1:10–12 applies this to Christ: the universe ages; God does not. Christ’s eternality proved from this psalm.

refs.note: Hebrews quotes verses 25-27 to demonstrate the Son's eternal nature over against created heavens.
Heavens vanishing like smoke and earth wearing out parallels the psalm's imagery.
The first heaven and earth passing away fulfills the psalm's transience description.

hist.context: The remarkable section (vv.25-27) contrasts the psalmist's brief life with God's eternal existence. Heaven and earth will perish, but God remains. Hebrews applies this to Christ, arguing the Son's superiority to angels through his eternal, unchanging nature. The psalm thus bridges individual lament and cosmic christology.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: God is a key figure in A Prayer of the Afflicted.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVI am like a desert owl, like an owl among the ruins.ESVI am like a desert owl of the wilderness, like an owl of the waste places;NIVDo not hide your face from me when I am in distress. Turn your ear to me; when I call, answer me quickly.ESVDo not hide your face from me in the day of my distress! Incline your ear to me; answer me speedily in the day when I call!

rec.text: ""Hear my prayer, LORD. Do not hide your face from me when I am in distress. Turn your ear to me; when I call, answer me quickly." Four urgent requests in two verses."

rows.label: Smoke and Ash — 102:1–11
rows.text: Days like smoke. Heart like grass. Desert owl. "You have thrown me aside."

hebtext.note: "A prayer of an afflicted (ʿānî) person who has grown weak and pours out a lament before the LORD" (superscription). The fifth penitential psalm. Days vanish like smoke, bones burn (v.3). The psalmist is like an owl in the desert, a bird alone on a roof (vv.6–7). Enemies use his name as a curse (v.8). He eats ashes and mingles his drink with tears (v.9). "Because of your great wrath... you have thrown me aside" (v.10).

hebtext.gloss: from generation to generation / age after age
hebtext.note: "But you, LORD, sit enthroned forever; your renown endures through all generations (lədōr wā-dōr)" (v.12). The pivot: from human transience to divine permanence. God will arise and have compassion on Zion (v.13). "He will respond to the prayer of the destitute" (v.17). The future nations will praise God (v.18). themes.note: Prophecy peaks: Hebrews 1:10–12 applies vv.25–27 to Christ’s eternality. Sovereignty: the universe ages; God remains. Mercy: God responds to the destitute.

prayer_prompt: An afflicted person pours out lament, but the psalm turns to God's eternal reign. Consider asking God about the laments you need to pour out — and the eternal perspective that frames them.
